 {"id":"bd-3gj","title":"Implement OperationProgress bar for determinate operations","description":"## Purpose
Create a progress bar renderable for operations where we know the total count, showing completion percentage, throughput, and ETA.

## Background
Bulk operations (batch inserts, data exports, migrations) need progress feedback. Users want to see:
- How much is done vs remaining
- Current throughput
- Estimated time to completion
- Running elapsed time

## Implementation Details

### File Location
crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/operation_progress.rs

### Core Struct
OperationProgress holds:
- completed: u64
- total: u64
- started_at: Instant
- operation_name: String
- theme: Theme
- width: Option<usize>
- show_eta: bool
- show_throughput: bool

### Rendering (Rich Mode)
Uses rich_rust ProgressBar with customizations:
- Colored bar based on completion percentage
- Percentage display
- Items processed counter
- Throughput calculation
- ETA based on current rate

Format: Inserting rows [===>    ] 42% (420/1000) 50.2/s ETA: 12s

### Throughput Calculation
rate = completed / elapsed_seconds
eta_seconds = (total - completed) / rate

### Style Variations
- Standard: Blue bar
- Success (100%): Green bar
- Warning (slow): Yellow bar
- Error (stalled): Red bar

### Plain Text (Agent Mode)
Inserting rows: 42% (420/1000) 50.2 rows/s ETA: 12s

## API Design
OperationProgress::new(name, total)
  .completed(n)  // or .increment() / .add(n)
  .theme(theme)
  .show_eta(true)
  .show_throughput(true)
  .width(60)
  .render(width) / .render_plain()

### Update Pattern (for live progress)
let mut progress = OperationProgress::new("Inserting", 1000);
for batch in batches {
    // do work
    progress.add(batch.len());
    console.update_progress(&progress);
}

## Verification Steps
1. Test at 0%, 50%, 100%
2. Verify throughput calculation accuracy
3. Verify ETA reasonableness
4. Test with very fast operations (sub-second)
5. Test with very slow operations (minutes)
6. Verify plain text parseable format
7. Test width constraints

## Dependencies
- rich_rust ProgressBar component
- Theme from this crate
- std::time::Instant for timing","acceptance_criteria":"OperationProgress shows percentage completion

 {"id":"bd-3j0","title":"Add sqlmodel-console to workspace Cargo.toml","description":"# Add sqlmodel-console to Workspace Cargo.toml

## Task Description

Register the new `sqlmodel-console` crate in the workspace root Cargo.toml so it's
recognized as part of the sqlmodel_rust workspace.

## Changes Required

### In /data/projects/sqlmodel_rust/Cargo.toml

Add to the `[workspace]` members list:
```toml
[workspace]
members = [
    "crates/sqlmodel",
    "crates/sqlmodel-core",
    "crates/sqlmodel-macros",
    "crates/sqlmodel-query",
    "crates/sqlmodel-schema",
    "crates/sqlmodel-pool",
    "crates/sqlmodel-postgres",
    "crates/sqlmodel-sqlite",
    "crates/sqlmodel-mysql",
    "crates/sqlmodel-console",  # ADD THIS LINE
]
```

### Optional: Add to Workspace Dependencies

If we want other crates to depend on sqlmodel-console, add:
```toml
[workspace.dependencies]
sqlmodel-console = { path = "crates/sqlmodel-console" }
```

## Verification

```bash
# From workspace root
cargo check --workspace

# Specifically check the new crate
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console

# Verify it appears in workspace
cargo metadata --format-version=1 | jq '.workspace_members[] | select(contains("sqlmodel-console"))'
```

## Important Notes

- The workspace Cargo.toml is the source of truth for workspace membership
- Order in members list doesn't matter but alphabetical is nice
- Don't add features to workspace.dependencies yet; let each crate specify what it needs
- This task cannot be done until the crate directory and Cargo.toml exist

## Why This Matters

Without workspace registration:
- `cargo check --workspace` won't include the crate
- `cargo test --workspace` won't run its tests
- IDE tooling may not recognize it
- Other crates can't depend on it via workspace path","acceptance_criteria":"sqlmodel-console added to workspace members

 {"id":"bd-59d","title":"Create SchemaTree renderable for database structure visualization","description":"## Purpose
Implement a tree renderable that displays database schema structure in a hierarchical format, perfect for visualizing table relationships, columns, indexes, and constraints.

## Background
When users inspect database schemas, they need to see:
- Tables as tree nodes
- Columns with types and constraints as children
- Indexes and foreign keys as nested items
- Visual distinction between primary keys, nullable, indexed columns

This uses rich_rust's Tree component to create an intuitive visual representation.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
`crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/schema_tree.rs`

### Core Struct
```rust
use rich_rust::prelude::*;
use sqlmodel_schema::{TableSchema, ColumnInfo, IndexInfo, ForeignKey};

/// Configuration for schema tree rendering
pub struct SchemaTreeConfig {
    /// Show column types inline
    pub show_types: bool,
    /// Show constraints (PK, FK, NOT NULL)
    pub show_constraints: bool,
    /// Show indexes
    pub show_indexes: bool,
    /// Color theme
    pub theme: Theme,
}

impl Default for SchemaTreeConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            show_types: true,
            show_constraints: true,
            show_indexes: true,
            theme: Theme::default(),
        }
    }
}

/// Renders a database schema as a tree structure
pub struct SchemaTree<'a> {
    tables: &'a [TableSchema],
    config: SchemaTreeConfig,
    title: Option<String>,
}

impl<'a> SchemaTree<'a> {
    pub fn new(tables: &'a [TableSchema]) -> Self {
        Self {
            tables,
            config: SchemaTreeConfig::default(),
            title: None,
        }
    }

    pub fn with_config(mut self, config: SchemaTreeConfig) -> Self {
        self.config = config;
        self
    }

    pub fn title(mut self, title: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.title = Some(title.into());
        self
    }

    /// Build tree node for a single table
    fn build_table_node(&self, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let table_label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.table_name_color(),
            table.name
        );
        let mut node = TreeNode::new(&table_label);

        // Add columns
        for col in &table.columns {
            let col_node = self.build_column_node(col, table);
            node.add_child(col_node);
        }

        // Add indexes if enabled
        if self.config.show_indexes {
            for idx in &table.indexes {
                let idx_node = self.build_index_node(idx);
                node.add_child(idx_node);
            }
        }

        node
    }

    fn build_column_node(&self, col: &ColumnInfo, table: &TableSchema) -> TreeNode {
        let mut parts = vec\![col.name.clone()];

        if self.config.show_types {
            parts.push(format\!(
                "[{}]{}[/]",
                self.config.theme.type_color(),
                col.sql_type
            ));
        }

        if self.config.show_constraints {
            let mut constraints = Vec::new();
            if col.is_primary_key {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]PK[/]",
                    self.config.theme.primary_key_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!col.nullable {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]NOT NULL[/]",
                    self.config.theme.constraint_color()
                ));
            }
            if col.has_default {
                constraints.push(format\!(
                    "[{}]DEFAULT[/]",
                    self.config.theme.default_color()
                ));
            }
            if \!constraints.is_empty() {
                parts.push(constraints.join(" "));
            }
        }

        TreeNode::new(&parts.join(" "))
    }

    fn build_index_node(&self, idx: &IndexInfo) -> TreeNode {
        let idx_type = if idx.unique { "UNIQUE" } else { "INDEX" };
        let label = format\!(
            "[{}]{}[/] {} ({})",
            self.config.theme.index_color(),
            idx_type,
            idx.name,
            idx.columns.join(", ")
        );
        TreeNode::new(&label)
    }

    /// Render to segments for rich mode
    pub fn render(&self, width: usize) -> Vec<Segment> {
        let root_label = self.title.clone()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| "Database Schema".to_string());
        let mut root = TreeNode::new(&format\!(
            "[bold {}]{}[/]",
            self.config.theme.heading_color(),
            root_label
        ));

        for table in self.tables {
            root.add_child(self.build_table_node(table));
        }

        let tree = Tree::new(root);
        tree.render(width)
    }

    /// Render to plain text for agent mode
    pub fn render_plain(&self) -> String {
        let mut output = String::new();
        let title = self.title.as_deref().unwrap_or("Database Schema");
        output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", title));
        output.push_str(&"=".repeat(title.len()));
        output.push(n);

        for table in self.tables {
            output.push_str(&format\!("\nTable: {}\n", table.name));
            for col in &table.columns {
                let mut line = format\!("  - {}: {}", col.name, col.sql_type);
                if col.is_primary_key {
                    line.push_str(" [PK]");
                }
                if \!col.nullable {
                    line.push_str(" NOT NULL");
                }
                output.push_str(&format\!("{}\n", line));
            }
        }
        output
    }
}
```

## Usage Example
```rust
let tables = vec\![user_schema, post_schema];
let tree = SchemaTree::new(&tables)
    .title("my_database")
    .with_config(SchemaTreeConfig {
        show_indexes: true,
        ..Default::default()
    });

console.print_renderable(&tree);
```

## Visual Output (Rich Mode)
```
📊 my_database
├── users
│   ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
│   ├── name TEXT NOT NULL
│   ├── email TEXT NOT NULL
│   └── UNIQUE idx_users_email (email)
└── posts
    ├── id INTEGER PK NOT NULL
    ├── user_id INTEGER NOT NULL
    ├── title TEXT NOT NULL
    └── INDEX idx_posts_user (user_id)
```

## Verification Steps
1. Create test schema with multiple tables
2. Verify tree renders correctly in rich mode
3. Verify plain text output for agent mode
4. Test with various config options
5. Ensure colors match Theme settings
6. Test with empty schema (no tables)
7. Test with table with no indexes

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-schema for TableSchema, ColumnInfo types
- rich_rust Tree and TreeNode components

-{"id":"bd-88i","title":"Phase 7: Database Driver Console Integration","description":"## Purpose
Integrate the console output system into each database driver crate, providing rich feedback for connection establishment, query execution, and driver-specific operations.

## Background
Each driver (PostgreSQL, SQLite, MySQL) has unique operations that benefit from rich output:
- Connection establishment with progress/status
- Authentication feedback (especially multi-step like SCRAM)
- Query timing and explain plan visualization
- Driver-specific features (PostgreSQL COPY, SQLite pragmas, MySQL procedures)

## Key Deliverables

### 1. sqlmodel-postgres Console Integration
- Connection establishment progress (DNS resolve -> TCP connect -> SSL -> Auth)
- SCRAM authentication step visualization
- Query timing display
- EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE visualization with tree rendering
- COPY progress for bulk operations

### 2. sqlmodel-sqlite Console Integration
- Database file operations (open, create, attach)
- PRAGMA visualization
- Transaction state indicator
- WAL checkpoint progress

### 3. sqlmodel-mysql Console Integration
- Connection establishment with capability negotiation
- Authentication plugin feedback
- Query timing
- SHOW commands formatting

## Integration Pattern
Each driver implements ConsoleAware trait:
- fn set_console(&mut self, console: Option<Arc<SqlModelConsole>>)
- Operations check if console is Some and output mode is Rich
- All output goes to stderr (non-semantic)
- Plain mode outputs nothing or minimal text

## Agent Safety
All driver console output:
1. Goes to stderr only (stdout reserved for query results)
2. Can be disabled via output mode
3. Plain mode emits parseable text only

## Dependencies
- Phase 2 (Core Infrastructure) - SqlModelConsole
- Phase 6 (Pool & Operations) - progress components
- Respective driver crates must be ready

## Verification
- Test each driver with console enabled/disabled
- Verify agent mode produces no rich output
- Performance test (console output must not slow queries)","acceptance_criteria":"PostgreSQL driver implements ConsoleAware trait

 {"id":"bd-9b5","title":"Create visual example programs for console features","description":"## Purpose
Create example programs that demonstrate and visually verify all console renderables, serving as both documentation and visual regression tests.

## Background
Visual examples serve multiple purposes:
1. Documentation for users
2. Visual regression testing
3. Development debugging
4. Feature showcasing

## Implementation Details

### Example Programs

#### examples/console_demo.rs
Comprehensive demo of all renderables:
- ErrorPanel with various error types
- QueryResultTable with sample data
- SchemaTree with multi-table schema
- Progress bars at different states
- Themed output variations

#### examples/error_showcase.rs
All error panel variations:
- Connection errors (with context)
- Query errors (with SQL highlight)
- Validation errors (with suggestions)
- Internal errors (with debug info)

#### examples/query_results.rs

- Wide result set (needs horizontal scroll)
- Long result set (needs vertical pagination)

- Unicode content handling

#### examples/progress_demo.rs
Progress indicator showcase:
- Determinate progress at various %
- Indeterminate spinners (all styles)
- Batch operation tracker
- Pool status dashboard
- Combined multi-progress display

#### examples/schema_visualization.rs
Schema display variations:
- Single table (TableInfo panel)
- Multi-table schema (SchemaTree)

- DDL syntax highlighting

Each example follows pattern:
fn main() {

    // Plain mode comparison

    let plain_console = SqlModelConsole::plain();
    plain_console.print_renderable(&renderable);
}

### Running Examples
cargo run --example console_demo --features console
cargo run --example error_showcase --features console
...

### Screenshot Documentation
Each example should be run and screenshot captured for docs:
- Terminal with dark theme
- Terminal with light theme
- Different terminal widths

## Verification Steps
1. Each example compiles and runs
2. Visual output matches expectations
3. Plain mode output is parseable
4. Examples work with/without TTY
5. Examples serve as regression tests

- Theme system complete

 {"id":"bd-ahh","title":"Create TableInfo panel for single-table details","description":"## Purpose
Implement a Panel-based renderable that displays comprehensive information about a single database table, including columns, indexes, foreign keys, and statistics.

## Background
When users want to inspect a specific table in detail (similar to psql \d tablename), they need:
- Table name and metadata in a header
- Column details in a formatted table
- Index information
- Foreign key relationships
- Table statistics (row count, size if available)

This is complementary to SchemaTree - SchemaTree shows the overview, TableInfo shows the details.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/table_info.rs

### Key Components
1. TableStats struct - holds optional runtime statistics
2. TableInfo struct - main renderable with schema reference
3. Builder methods for theme, width, stats
4. Internal helpers to build columns table, indexes section, FK section
5. render() method for rich mode using Panel + Table
6. render_plain() method for agent mode

### Features
- Columns displayed in a rich_rust Table within a Panel
- Shows column name, type, nullable, default, constraints
- Indexes section with UNIQUE/INDEX type and columns
- Foreign keys showing local->remote.column relationships
- Optional stats: row count (with thousand separators), size (formatted as KB/MB/GB)

### Helper Functions
- format_number(): Add thousand separators to numbers
- format_bytes(): Convert bytes to human-readable KB/MB/GB

## Usage Example
let table_info = TableInfo::new(&user_table_schema)
    .with_stats(TableStats {
        row_count: Some(10_000),
        size_bytes: Some(2_500_000),
        ..Default::default()
    })
    .width(80);
console.print_renderable(&table_info);

## Verification Steps
1. Test with table having all features (indexes, FKs, defaults)
2. Test with minimal table (just columns)
3. Verify plain text output is parseable
4. Test width constraints
5. Verify stats formatting
6. Test with empty table schema

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-schema for TableSchema types
- rich_rust Panel and Table components
- Theme from this crate","acceptance_criteria":"TableInfo panel shows table name and schema
Panel shows all columns with types
Panel shows primary key information

fn test_query_results_go_to_stdout() {
    // Capture stdout/stderr

```rust
//! Query result table renderable.

use crate::theme::Theme;
use sqlmodel_core::{Row, Value};
use std::time::Duration;

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::prelude::*;

/// Options for query result display.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct QueryResultOptions {
    /// Maximum rows to display (None = all)
    pub max_rows: Option<usize>,
    /// Maximum column width before truncation
    pub max_column_width: usize,
    /// Show row numbers
    pub show_row_numbers: bool,
    /// Truncation indicator
    pub truncation_indicator: String,
    /// Show timing information
    pub show_timing: bool,
    /// Plain mode output format
    pub plain_format: PlainFormat,
}

/// Output format for plain mode.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Default)]
pub enum PlainFormat {
    /// Pipe-delimited: id|name|value
    #[default]
    Pipe,
    /// CSV with proper quoting
    Csv,
    /// JSON Lines (one object per row)
    JsonLines,
    /// JSON array of objects
    JsonArray,
}

impl Default for QueryResultOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_rows: Some(100),
            max_column_width: 50,
            show_row_numbers: false,
            truncation_indicator: "...".to_string(),
            show_timing: true,
            plain_format: PlainFormat::Pipe,
        }
    }
}

/// A table renderable for query results.
pub struct QueryResultTable {
    /// Column names
    columns: Vec<String>,
    /// Row data
    rows: Vec<Vec<Value>>,
    /// Query execution time
    timing: Option<Duration>,
    /// Display options
    options: QueryResultOptions,
}

impl QueryResultTable {
    /// Create from column names and rows.
    pub fn new(columns: Vec<String>, rows: Vec<Vec<Value>>) -> Self {
        Self {
            columns,
            rows,
            timing: None,
            options: QueryResultOptions::default(),
        }
    }
    
    /// Create from sqlmodel_core Rows.
    pub fn from_rows(column_names: &[String], rows: &[Row]) -> Self {
        let data: Vec<Vec<Value>> = rows.iter()
            .map(|row| row.values().cloned().collect())
            .collect();
        Self::new(column_names.to_vec(), data)
    }
    
    /// Set query timing.
    pub fn with_timing(mut self, timing: Duration) -> Self {
        self.timing = Some(timing);
        self
    }
    
    /// Set display options.
    pub fn with_options(mut self, options: QueryResultOptions) -> Self {
        self.options = options;
        self
    }
    
    /// Set plain format.
    pub fn plain_format(mut self, format: PlainFormat) -> Self {
        self.options.plain_format = format;
        self
    }
    
    /// Row count.
    pub fn row_count(&self) -> usize {
        self.rows.len()
    }
    
    /// Render as rich Table.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_table(&self, theme: &Theme) -> Panel {
        let mut table = Table::new();
        
        // Add columns
        for col in &self.columns {
            table = table.with_column(
                Column::new(col)
                    .header_style(theme.header.to_style().bold())
            );
        }
        
        // Add rows (respecting max_rows)
        let display_rows = match self.options.max_rows {
            Some(max) => self.rows.iter().take(max).collect::<Vec<_>>(),
            None => self.rows.iter().collect::<Vec<_>>(),
        };
        
        for row_data in &display_rows {
            let mut row = rich_rust::Row::new();
            for value in *row_data {
                let (text, style) = self.format_value(value, theme);
                row = row.cell(Cell::new(&text).style(style));
            }
            table.add_row(row);
        }
        
        // Build header with timing
        let header = if let Some(timing) = self.timing {
            format!(
                "{} rows in {:.2}ms",
                self.rows.len(),
                timing.as_secs_f64() * 1000.0
            )
        } else {
            format!("{} rows", self.rows.len())
        };
        
        // Truncation notice
        let footer = if self.options.max_rows.map_or(false, |m| self.rows.len() > m) {
            Some(format!(
                "... and {} more rows",
                self.rows.len() - self.options.max_rows.unwrap()
            ))
        } else {
            None
        };
        
        let mut panel = Panel::new(table)
            .title("Query Results")
            .subtitle(&header);
        
        if let Some(f) = footer {
            panel = panel.subtitle_bottom(&f);
        }
        
        panel
    }
    
    /// Format a value for display with styling.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    fn format_value(&self, value: &Value, theme: &Theme) -> (String, Style) {
        match value {
            Value::Null => ("NULL".to_string(), theme.null_value.to_style().italic()),
            Value::Bool(b) => (b.to_string(), theme.bool_value.to_style()),
            Value::TinyInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::SmallInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Int(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::BigInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Float(f) => (format!("{:.4}", f), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Double(d) => (format!("{:.6}", d), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Text(s) => (
                self.truncate_string(s),
                theme.string_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Bytes(b) => (
                format!("[BLOB: {} bytes]", b.len()),
                theme.binary_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Date(_) | Value::Time(_) | Value::Timestamp(_) | Value::TimestampTz(_) => (
                format!("{}", value),
                theme.date_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Uuid(bytes) => (
                format_uuid(bytes),
                theme.uuid_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Json(j) => (
                self.truncate_string(&j.to_string()),
                theme.json_value.to_style()
            ),
            _ => (format!("{}", value), Style::new()),
        }
    }
    
    fn truncate_string(&self, s: &str) -> String {
        if s.len() > self.options.max_column_width {
            format!(
                "{}{}",
                &s[..self.options.max_column_width - self.options.truncation_indicator.len()],
                self.options.truncation_indicator
            )
        } else {
            s.to_string()
        }
    }
    
    /// Render as plain text.
    pub fn to_plain(&self) -> String {
        match self.options.plain_format {
            PlainFormat::Pipe => self.to_pipe_delimited(),
            PlainFormat::Csv => self.to_csv(),
            PlainFormat::JsonLines => self.to_json_lines(),
            PlainFormat::JsonArray => self.to_json_array(),
        }
    }
    
    fn to_pipe_delimited(&self) -> String {
        let mut lines = Vec::new();
        lines.push(self.columns.join("|"));
        for row in &self.rows {
            let values: Vec<String> = row.iter()
                .map(|v| format!("{}", v))
                .collect();
            lines.push(values.join("|"));
        }
        lines.join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_csv(&self) -> String {
        let mut lines = Vec::new();
        lines.push(self.columns.iter()
            .map(|c| csv_quote(c))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(","));
        for row in &self.rows {
            let values: Vec<String> = row.iter()
                .map(|v| csv_quote(&format!("{}", v)))
                .collect();
            lines.push(values.join(","));
        }
        lines.join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_json_lines(&self) -> String {
        self.rows.iter()
            .map(|row| {
                let obj: serde_json::Map<String, serde_json::Value> = 
                    self.columns.iter().zip(row.iter())
                        .map(|(col, val)| (col.clone(), value_to_json(val)))
                        .collect();
                serde_json::to_string(&obj).unwrap_or_default()
            })
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_json_array(&self) -> String {
        let arr: Vec<serde_json::Map<String, serde_json::Value>> = self.rows.iter()
            .map(|row| {
                self.columns.iter().zip(row.iter())
                    .map(|(col, val)| (col.clone(), value_to_json(val)))
                    .collect()
            })
            .collect();
        serde_json::to_string_pretty(&arr).unwrap_or_default()
    }
}

fn csv_quote(s: &str) -> String {
    if s.contains(',') || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') {
        format!("\"{}\"", s.replace('"', "\"\""))
    } else {
        s.to_string()
    }
}

fn value_to_json(v: &Value) -> serde_json::Value {
    match v {
        Value::Null => serde_json::Value::Null,
        Value::Bool(b) => serde_json::Value::Bool(*b),
        Value::BigInt(n) => serde_json::json!(n),
        Value::Int(n) => serde_json::json!(n),
        Value::Double(d) => serde_json::json!(d),
        Value::Text(s) => serde_json::Value::String(s.clone()),
        _ => serde_json::Value::String(format!("{}", v)),
    }
}

fn format_uuid(bytes: &[u8; 16]) -> String {
    format!(
        "{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}",
        bytes[0], bytes[1], bytes[2], bytes[3],
        bytes[4], bytes[5], bytes[6], bytes[7],
        bytes[8], bytes[9], bytes[10], bytes[11],
        bytes[12], bytes[13], bytes[14], bytes[15]
    )
}
```

 {"id":"bd-tel","title":"Write sqlmodel-console Cargo.toml with feature flags","description":"# Write sqlmodel-console Cargo.toml with Feature Flags

## Task Description

Create the Cargo.toml for sqlmodel-console with proper feature flags that make
rich_rust an optional dependency. This is critical for the "zero cost when disabled"
design principle.

## Cargo.toml Content

```toml
[package]
name = "sqlmodel-console"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"
rust-version = "1.85"
authors = ["Jeffrey Emanuel <jeff@pastel.network>"]
description = "Beautiful terminal output for sqlmodel_rust"
license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"
repository = "https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/sqlmodel_rust"
keywords = ["database", "console", "terminal", "rich", "styled"]
categories = ["command-line-interface", "visualization"]

[features]
default = []

# Basic rich output (tables, panels, colors)
rich = ["dep:rich_rust"]

# SQL syntax highlighting (adds syntect dependency via rich_rust)
syntax = ["rich", "rich_rust/syntax"]

# Full feature set
full = ["rich", "syntax"]

[dependencies]
# Core (always available)
serde = { version = "1.0", features = ["derive"] }

# Optional: rich terminal output
rich_rust = { path = "../../../rich_rust", optional = true }

[dev-dependencies]
# For testing output capture
# (none needed initially)
```

## Key Design Decisions

### Why Path Dependency for rich_rust?
1. Both projects are developed together
2. Avoids version sync issues with crates.io
3. Local changes immediately available
4. No network dependency during development

### Why `dep:rich_rust` Syntax?
Rust 2021+ allows `dep:` prefix to reference optional dependencies in feature 
definitions without implicitly creating a feature with the same name.

### Feature Hierarchy
```
full ──► syntax ──► rich ──► [rich_rust crate]
```

Each feature level adds capabilities:
- `rich`: Basic Console, colors, tables, panels, trees
- `syntax`: SQL syntax highlighting via syntect
- `full`: All features enabled

## Verification

```bash
# Check Cargo.toml syntax
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console 2>&1 || echo "Expected: won't work until workspace updated"

# After workspace update:
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features rich
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features syntax
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features full
```

## Notes

- Path to rich_rust is `../../../rich_rust` because:
  - From `crates/sqlmodel-console/` we go up to `crates/`
  - Then up to `sqlmodel_rust/`
  - Then up to parent containing both projects
  - Then into `rich_rust/`
  - Adjust if your directory structure differs

- No workspace inheritance for dependencies here since rich_rust is local","acceptance_criteria":"Cargo.toml has correct package metadata
Feature flags defined (rich, syntax, full)
rich_rust is optional path dependency
serde is optional for theme serialization
Dependencies version-locked appropriately
Package compiles with all feature combinations","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:03:05.244443813Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-19T21:43:44.268197649Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cargo","phase-1","rich-rust"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-tel","depends_on_id":"bd-1a2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:03:11.305055450Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-tel","depends_on_id":"bd-1vz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:03:05.277062919Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-u12","title":"Phase 4: Query Output System","description":"# Phase 4: Query Output System

## Overview

This phase implements beautiful query result display - tables for result sets,
SQL syntax highlighting for generated queries, and query structure visualization.

## Why Query Output Matters

Query results are the primary output of a database library. Beautiful result
tables transform the experience from "reading raw data" to "understanding data."

### Before (Plain)
```
id|name|email|created_at
1|Alice|alice@example.com|2024-01-15 10:30:00
2|Bob|bob@example.com|2024-01-16 14:22:00
```

### After (Rich)
```
┌──────────────────────────── Query Results ────────────────────────────┐
│ 2 rows in 12.34ms                                                     │
├───────┬─────────────┬──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────┤
│    id │ name        │ email                │ created_at              │
├───────┼─────────────┼──────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┤
│     1 │ Alice       │ alice@example.com    │ 2024-01-15 10:30:00    │
│     2 │ Bob         │ bob@example.com      │ 2024-01-16 14:22:00    │
└───────┴─────────────┴──────────────────────┴─────────────────────────┘
```

## Components to Implement

### 1. QueryResultTable Renderable
A table specifically for query results with:
- Auto-sized columns based on content
- Type-based cell coloring (nulls gray, numbers cyan, etc.)
- Row count and timing in header/footer
- Truncation for long values
- Optional row numbers

### 2. Value Type Styling
Color values based on their SQL type:
- `NULL` → Gray, italic
- `Boolean` → Yellow
- `Integer/Float` → Cyan
- `String` → Green
- `Date/Time` → Magenta
- `Binary/Blob` → Orange (show "[BLOB: 1234 bytes]")
- `JSON` → Purple

### 3. SQL Syntax Highlighting
Use rich_rust's syntax highlighting for SQL:
- Keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE) → Magenta
- Strings → Green
- Numbers → Purple
- Comments → Gray
- Operators → Red
- Identifiers → White

### 4. Query Builder Visualization
Tree view of query structure:
```
SELECT from heroes
├── Columns: id, name, secret_name
├── WHERE: age > 18
├── ORDER BY: name ASC
└── LIMIT: 10
```

### 5. Query Timing Display
Visual breakdown of query execution:
```
Query completed in 12.34ms (3 rows)
  Parse:  ██░░░░░░░░ 1.2ms
  Plan:   ████░░░░░░ 3.4ms  
  Exec:   ████████░░ 7.7ms
```

## Plain Mode Output Formats

For agent compatibility, plain mode supports:
1. **Pipe-delimited**: `id|name|email` (default)
2. **CSV**: Standard CSV with quoting
3. **JSON Lines**: One JSON object per row
4. **JSON Array**: Single array of objects

The format is selectable via `QueryResultTable::format()`.

## Tasks in This Phase

1. Create QueryResultTable renderable
2. Implement Value type styling system
3. Integrate SQL syntax highlighting
4. Create QueryTreeView for query structure
5. Implement QueryTiming display
6. Add methods to sqlmodel-query Select/Insert/Update/Delete
7. Create examples and tests

## Integration with sqlmodel-query

Add console display methods to query builders:

```rust
impl<M: Model> Select<M> {
    /// Print the generated SQL with syntax highlighting.
    #[cfg(feature = "console")]
    pub fn explain(&self, console: &SqlModelConsole, dialect: Dialect) {
        let sql = self.to_sql(dialect);
        console.print_sql(&sql);
    }
    
    /// Print query structure as tree.
    #[cfg(feature = "console")]
    pub fn print_structure(&self, console: &SqlModelConsole) {
        let tree = self.to_tree();
        console.print_tree(&tree);
    }
}
```

## Dependencies (Beads)

This phase depends on:
- Phase 2 (Core Infrastructure) - SqlModelConsole
- Phase 3 (Error Display) - for error handling in display

## Performance Considerations

- Column width calculation should be O(rows × cols)
- Cache column widths after first calculation
- Truncate long values rather than wrapping (configurable)
- Limit displayed rows (e.g., first 100) with "... and N more" indicator","acceptance_criteria":"QueryResultTable renderable displays query results in table format

Plain mode outputs parseable tabular data

All unit tests pass for query output

 {"id":"bd-vz2","title":"Integrate console into sqlmodel-postgres driver","description":"## Purpose
Add rich console output to the PostgreSQL driver for connection progress, authentication feedback, and query visualization.

## Background
PostgreSQL connections involve multiple steps:
1. DNS resolution
2. TCP connection
3. SSL/TLS negotiation (optional)
4. Authentication (password, SCRAM-SHA-256, etc)
5. Parameter negotiation

Each step can be visualized to show progress and help debug connection issues.

## Implementation Details

### File Modifications
crates/sqlmodel-postgres/src/connection.rs
crates/sqlmodel-postgres/src/lib.rs (re-exports)

### ConsoleAware Implementation
Add optional SqlModelConsole to PostgresConnection:
struct PostgresConnection {
    // existing fields...
    console: Option<Arc<SqlModelConsole>>,
}

impl ConsoleAware for PostgresConnection {
    fn set_console(&mut self, console: Option<Arc<SqlModelConsole>>) {
        self.console = console;
    }
}

### Connection Progress Output
When console is Some and mode is Rich:

Connecting to PostgreSQL...
  [OK] DNS resolved: 192.168.1.100
  [OK] TCP connected
  [..] Negotiating SSL...
  [OK] SSL established (TLS 1.3)
  [..] Authenticating (SCRAM-SHA-256)...
  [OK] Authenticated as user
  [OK] Connected (server: PostgreSQL 15.2)

### Query Timing Output
For queries, optionally show timing:
Query executed in 12.3ms (5 rows returned)

### EXPLAIN Visualization
When EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE is run:
- Parse the explain output
- Render as a tree showing:
  - Node types (Seq Scan, Index Scan, Hash Join)
  - Costs and actual times
  - Row estimates vs actual
  - Highlighting expensive nodes

### COPY Progress
For COPY operations:
- Show progress bar with row count
- Display throughput (rows/sec, MB/s)

## Plain Text Mode
In plain mode, emit minimal text:
Connected to PostgreSQL 15.2 at localhost:5432
Query: 12.3ms, 5 rows

## Conditional Compilation
Use cfg feature flag:
#[cfg(feature = "console")]
fn emit_connection_progress(&self, stage: &str, status: StageStatus) { ... }

#[cfg(not(feature = "console"))]
fn emit_connection_progress(&self, _: &str, _: StageStatus) {}

## Verification Steps
1. Test connection with console enabled
2. Test connection with console disabled
3. Verify agent mode produces plain text only
4. Test EXPLAIN tree rendering
5. Test COPY progress
6. Verify SSL and auth step feedback
7. Test connection failure messaging

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-console crate
- SqlModelConsole, Theme, OperationProgress components","acceptance_criteria":"PostgreSQL driver implements ConsoleAware trait
